Now the second key of prospecting is to get yourself into massive rapport or actually better said to maintain and build massive rapport as you're actually qualifying someone. You see what happens is this you start off and you capture someone's imagination by sounding good using those tone out as we spoke about right in your opening script. They say, you know, this guy sounds pretty interesting so they want to open up to you right here some more. I'm wanting to see some information about what it is that you have to offer, whatever your outcome is for the script and we'll get into that later in the script building module. But then what happens is you transition from all of a sudden, you know, sounding good to saying, let me ask you a couple of quick questions and you say, you know, how much money do you have in the bank or you might say, you know, where do you live or how many sales do you have working for your own? Who's your supplier right now? You start asking these invasive questions right and typically what happens is this, as you start to invade someone's privacy, you essentially start falling out of rapport. So you need to be very, very aware that as you qualify that you tend to run this risk of, you know, starting to drift out of rapport and into this realm where you're almost like the inquisitor. Okay, now again, if I had a graph, let me show you a straight line graph here right now you have the open and you have the close above and below the line you have your boundaries right. You get into immediate rapport on both the conscious and unconscious level right and now here we are gathering intelligence but remember as we're gathering intelligence we're not only maintaining rapport, we're also looking to continue to build rapport and on what's called the justifier. Okay, which is essentially the reason why I'm asking them questions. So I would say, listen, just a couple of quick questions sir so I can best serve you or a couple of quick questions so I don't waste your time. That so is your justifier. Essentially it's your reason why you're asking them questions right. It's not you're just wasting their time, it's not for any just you know, underhanded reason you're asking them questions so I don't waste your time or so I can best serve you. Okay, so again I would always use a justifier and just to show you how powerful a justifier can be, I want to tell you a little story here. It's actually based on a study that was performed in the University of Arizona by a very very well known psychologist named Dr. Robert Cialdini and what he did is he had this really interesting study where he had a copy machine in the library, it was a very busy machine, it was usually four or five people in front of it right and he had some students go up with a stack of papers and try to cut the line when there were four or five people at the copy machine right. So what he did is he did this experiment, they waited to lose four people at the machine right, people came up with a stack of papers they said excuse me can I please cut the line and what they found was that 25% of the time they would get compliance. People would actually let them cut the line simply because they asked and that was there essentially their baseline result that if you just simply go up and ask people to cut the line one out of four times 25% of the time they'll let you cut the line. Next day they went back there, different people online now right and they used what's called a justifier. Essentially they came with a reason why they had to cut the line. So instead of just saying hey can I cut the line they said listen I need to cut the line because I'm in a rush I'm going to miss my class. Essentially a justification about why you let them cut the line. So watch what happens they go back now wait till there's four people on the line and when there is they come up to the front say excuse me would you mind if I cut the line listen I'm really in a rush I need to make these copies I'm going to miss my class. Guess what happened? 75% of the people let them cut the line. 300% increase in compliance just by using a justifier by giving someone a reason why you need to do something. You understand this? Now let me just go one step further here. They figured out maybe it's because it's a good reason like it's really a valid reason why everyone can relate to that they're all in college right I'm going to miss a class so people say okay fine do it it makes sense. So they went back and next day they said let's just come up with some ridiculous reason why just like say I have to cut the line because I really have to. So next day they went up there four people on the line they sent in their moles and they say excuse me you know do you mind if I cut the line I really really have to. Guess what percentage let them cut the line? 75% it didn't matter it didn't matter whether it was a good reason or a bad reason you just had a reason why that's the power of justifiers okay now actually this little experiment goes on a bit longer but I'm going to save it for the script building module because there's some stuff here with certain words that we use and trigger phrases that actually bypass the the conscious mind they go right to the unconscious mind so you can simply circumvent the logic centers of people's brains we'll we'll go through those when it comes to script building but for right now just understand the power of using justifiers you would never just say listen you know a couple of quick questions you'd say this is just a couple of quick questions so I don't waste your time or a couple of quick questions so I could best serve you. So those are your first two keys to qualifying first to identify needs and again you want to do that in a way that goes just beyond basic needs goes to ancillary needs beliefs values and everything else right and to find out their why or their whys okay and number two make sure you use your justifiers so that you can gain compliance and also stay in rapport while you're gathering that intelligence versus falling out of rapport and being viewed as the sort of grand inquisitor that's sort of just looking to extract information remember you're not an extractor of information you are the trusted advisor the trusted friend the family physician who goes there and says scope tell me what's wrong and he takes out his pen and his paper he's like that's your your body language your tonality that's the the positioning that you come from up here that you're entitled to ask questions you're literally obligated to ask questions because you need to find out exactly what their needs are where their pain is so you can help them solve it
